mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes-AMG SLC43 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned SLC43 engine (M276.822) typically costs £3,312–£4,500 supply only depending on power output (367 PS or 390 PS). A replacement M276.822 engine costs on average £3,312 in good condition. Labour costs for engine replacement typically range from £800 to £2,500. On average, a full engine replacement can run anywhere between £1,500 and £5,000. Fitting adds £1,200–£1,800. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your vehicle.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes-AMG SLC43 engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The M276 is generally considered a reliable engine. The C43 AMG with the same engine has been reported as 'super reliable' even at 90,000 miles. However, it has known weaknesses: oil leaks from turbocharger oil feed lines and camshaft adjuster solenoids, turbocharger wear, HPFP issues, and 9G‑Tronic transmission hesitation. Regular oil changes (every 8,000‑10,000 miles) are essential to maintain reliability.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes-AMG SLC43?

acceptedAnswer.text: The most common issues are oil leaks (turbo oil feed lines and camshaft adjuster solenoids), turbocharger wear (whining, wastegate rattle, loss of power), high‑pressure fuel pump failure (hard starting, rough running), and 9G‑Tronic transmission hesitation. The M276 engine is generally reliable when properly maintained.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing an SLC43 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value and condition. Against a tidy £15,000–£30,000 SLC43, a £3,312–£4,500 recon is 10‑30% of the car's value – often worth it on well‑maintained examples. If the engine has suffered catastrophic turbo failure or timing chain failure, a full engine replacement may be necessary. Get a full diagnostic assessment before committing. The SLC43 is a rare and collectable car, so well‑preserved examples may justify the investment.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my SLC43's engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: The SLC43 AMG uses the M276.822 engine code – a 3.0‑litre V6 bi‑turbo. The engine code is stamped on the engine block and listed on your V5C. The 367 PS variant was produced from 2016‑2018; the 390 PS variant was produced from 2018‑2020.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair an SLC43 engine issue, or does it need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: Most issues – oil leaks, turbocharger wear (if caught early), HPFP failure, transmission hesitation – are repairable. However, if the turbocharger has failed catastrophically and sent metal fragments into the engine, a full engine replacement is usually more cost‑effective than a rebuild. The M276 is a complex, high‑performance engine requiring specialist knowledge.

rows.bestFor: Insurance jobs; warranty claims; full provenance

replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect the SLC43 AMG pricing. A replacement M276.822 engine costs on average £3,312 in good condition, with new or reconditioned units costing up to £3,450. Labour costs for engine replacement typically range from £800 to £2,500. On average, a full engine replacement can run anywhere between £1,500 and £5,000 depending on the car model and engine type. As a handcrafted AMG engine, costs are at the upper end of the range. A full engine replacement with labour can cost £9,000–£13,000.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting an SLC43 engine typically takes 12‑16 hours at £75‑£110/hr (independent Mercedes specialist). The SLC's compact roadster engine bay and the twin‑turbo V6 layout add significant labour complexity.

cta.label: → Compare SLC43 AMG engine prices from UK specialists

problems.title: M276 Oil Leaks — Turbocharger Oil Feed Lines & Camshaft Adjuster Solenoids
problems.symptoms: Oil spots under the vehicle, burning oil smell, low oil level warning, visible oil leak from the engine bay. Oil leaks are a prevalent issue on the M276 engine. Both the turbocharger oil feed lines and the camshaft adjuster solenoids are known leak points.
problems.repairCost: £300–£1,200 [PLACEHOLDER] (oil feed line or solenoid seal replacement).
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (if caught early; ignored oil loss can lead to engine damage).

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if oil leak is detected.

problems.recommendation: Oil leaks are one of the most frequent complaints with the M276 engine. The turbocharger oil feed lines can develop leaks, and the camshaft adjuster solenoids can weep oil, which can then migrate through the wiring harness and cause sensor and control unit issues. If you notice oil spots or a burning smell, have the engine inspected immediately. Ignoring oil leaks can lead to accelerated engine wear, turbo failure, and in worst cases, bearing damage or engine seizure.

problems.title: Turbocharger Wear — Whining, Wastegate Rattle & Boost Issues
problems.symptoms: Whining or siren‑like noise from the engine bay that changes with RPM, rattling noise (wastegate rattle), loss of power, sluggish acceleration, excessive exhaust smoke (blue or grey), Check Engine Light with underboost codes (P0299).
problems.repairCost: £800–£2,500 [PLACEHOLDER] (turbocharger replacement — left and right turbos are different and not interchangeable).
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (if caught early; ignored turbo failure can send metal fragments into the engine).

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if you notice whining, power loss, or rattling.

problems.recommendation: The M276 engine uses two turbochargers — one for each cylinder bank. Oil starvation and contamination are the leading causes of failure. A wastegate rattle is a common issue, but excessive play can lead to boost leaks and requires replacement. If you notice a whining or rattling noise, loss of power, or smoke, have the turbo system inspected immediately. When replacing a turbo, always replace the oil feed lines and change the oil.

problems.title: High‑Pressure Fuel Pump (HPFP) & Fuel System Issues
problems.symptoms: Long cranking on start (hot or cold), loss of power, engine stalling, engine warning light, rough idle, misfires. Fuel system issues are a common cause of stalling on the SLC43 AMG.
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (if caught early; ignored HPFP failure can damage catalytic converters).

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if hard starting or rough running occurs.

problems.recommendation: The M276's direct injection system places high demands on the fuel system. A clogged fuel filter or failing fuel pump can restrict fuel flow, resulting in poor engine performance and starting issues. Injector driver faults and ECU issues have also been reported. If you experience hard starting or rough running, have the fuel system inspected immediately. Regular fuel filter changes and using high‑quality fuel can help prevent issues.

problems.title: 9G‑Tronic Transmission Hesitation & Shift Quality Issues
problems.symptoms: Hesitation when pulling away, jerky gear changes, transmission getting confused, delayed downshifts. The 9G‑Tronic has been described as "wonky" by some owners.
problems.repairCost: £500–£2,500 [PLACEHOLDER] (software update, valve body repair, or transmission overhaul).

urgency.label: Monitor
urgency.text: book inspection soon if symptoms develop. If the transmission hesitates dangerously when pulling into traffic, book an urgent inspection.

problems.recommendation: The AMG‑tuned 9G‑Tronic transmission in the SLC43 can suffer from hesitation and jerky shifts. A software update (TCU reprogramming) may resolve the issue. In more severe cases, valve body repair or clutch replacement may be required. If you experience hesitation when pulling away, have the transmission control module software checked.

problems.title: M276 Camshaft Adjuster Solenoid & Timing Chain Wear
problems.symptoms: Rattling or ticking noise on cold start, rough idle, engine warning light. Camshaft adjuster solenoids can wear, and timing chain stretch is a potential issue on neglected oil intervals.

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if you hear unusual noises on cold start.

problems.recommendation: The M276's camshaft adjuster solenoids can wear, causing ticking or rattling noises on cold start. Regular oil changes (using the correct MB‑spec oil) are essential to prevent premature wear. If you hear unusual noises on start‑up, have the camshaft adjusters inspected. A preventative solenoid replacement is far cheaper than addressing timing chain issues later.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: Mercedes-AMG SLC43 — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Worth fixing — common on high‑stress turbo engines
Worth fixing — common and well‑understood repair
Software update may resolve; worth fixing
Worth fixing — common on higher‑mileage engines
Full engine replacement often more cost‑effective

buyingChecks.title: Buying This Mercedes-AMG SLC43? Check These First
buyIf: * Full Mercedes service history with regular oil changes (every 8,000‑10,000 miles)
* No oil leaks — turbo oil feed lines and camshaft adjuster solenoids should be dry
* Turbo system in good health — no whining or rattling
* Transmission shifts smoothly — no hesitation or jerky shifts
* A well‑maintained example — the M276 is a durable engine when properly cared for
avoidIf: * Any sign of oil leaks — turbo lines or solenoid seals
* Any sign of turbo whining or rattling — turbocharger warning
* Any sign of hard starting or rough running — HPFP or injector warning
* Any sign of transmission hesitation or jerky shifts — 9G‑Tronic warning
* Poor service history — the M276 is intolerant of neglect

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The Mercedes-AMG SLC43 is a rare and characterful roadster — the final AMG‑powered two‑seat convertible before the SL‑Class re‑established the V8 lineage. The M276 3.0‑litre V6 bi‑turbo is a smooth, responsive engine that delivers 367‑390 PS and 520 Nm of torque. It is generally reliable, but like all high‑performance engines, it has known weaknesses: oil leaks from turbocharger oil feed lines and camshaft adjuster solenoids, turbocharger wear, HPFP issues, and 9G‑Tronic transmission hesitation. Buy on service history above all else — a well‑maintained SLC43 is a rewarding and increasingly collectable car; a neglected example can be expensive to repair.

cta.label: → Compare SLC43 AMG engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Mercedes-AMG SLC43 (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: 1. Cold Start — Any rattling or ticking? (camshaft adjuster warning)
2. Service History — Regular oil changes (every 8,000‑10,000 miles) — this is non‑negotiable
3. Oil Leaks — Check under the vehicle and around the engine for any signs of leakage
4. Turbo Health — Any whining, rattling, or loss of power? (turbocharger warning)
5. Hard Starting — Any long cranking on hot or cold start? (HPFP warning)
6. Transmission — Any hesitation, jerky shifts, or clunking? (9G‑Tronic warning)
7. Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Any turbo, fuel, or transmission codes?
8. MOT History — Check for emissions‑related failures or any advisory notes
